metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC4 to state a measurable commit-count target (e.g. 'ceil(N/256) commits, verified by asserting `git rev-list --count HEAD` before/after a synthetic N-event import fixture') and add a deterministic state-diff command (e.g. `rebar replay | sha256sum` comparison) as the parity proof.
findings.finding: AC4 uses 'far fewer commits' rather than a measurable target, and provides no proving command to count or bound commits after the cutover.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'A real import run produces the same final replayed state as the per-event path (byte/state parity) with far fewer commits'
findings.suggested_fix: AC4: 'A synthetic import of N events (N > 256) produces exactly ceil(N/256) commits (verified by `git rev-list --count HEAD` delta in the test fixture) and the replayed ticket state is byte-for-byte identical to a per-event reference run (verified by `rebar replay --format json | sha256sum` equality).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the `batch_stage_and_commit` primitive exists (or identify the correct existing function name) before the Scope can be executed; if it does not exist, add a Scope item to build the multi-event batch-commit primitive as part of this story.
findings.finding: The plan treats `batch_stage_and_commit` as an already-existing 'B1 batch primitive' to adopt, but no such function exists anywhere in the codebase. The only related function is `stage_and_commit` in `event_append.py`, which commits exactly one event under one lock acquisition — the same per-event pattern the plan is trying to replace. The 'B1' label in the codebase refers to the plan-review batch runner (`production_batch_runner.py`), which is entirely unrelated to the store write path.
findings.location: Plan § What / Why / Scope — 'Adopt the B1 batch primitive', 'flush through batch_stage_and_commit'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the referenced primitive to `stage_and_commit` (singular) if the intent is to call it N times within a single lock hold, OR add an explicit Scope bullet: 'Add `batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, events: list[tuple[ticket_id, event_dict]]) -> None` to `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` that acquires the write lock once and commits all events in a single `git commit`.' Make clear whether the multi-event batcher is new work in B2 or a prerequisite from a prior story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite statement that B1 (`batch_stage_and_commit` in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`, ticket 890d-4733-1c88-4976) must be merged before B2 work begins.
findings.finding: `batch_stage_and_commit` (the B1 primitive this plan routes through) does not yet exist in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`; B1 (ticket 890d-4733-1c88-4976) is still open/unimplemented. The plan names B2 as depending on B1's primitive but does not state this as an explicit prerequisite, creating a hidden hard dependency.
findings.location: Scope section / AC: 'buffer composed events and flush via `batch_stage_and_commit` in 256-event chunks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites' section: 'B1 (ticket 890d-4733-1c88-4976) must be merged and `batch_stage_and_commit` present in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` before this task starts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how Pass 2b (links) is routed through `batch_stage_and_commit`: either acknowledge that `add_dependency`'s stateful pre-commit reads prevent true batching for Pass 2b (making it per-event like 2e), or enumerate `src/rebar/graph/_links.py` as an edit target with a compose-only variant of `_write_link_event`.
findings.finding: Pass 2b (links) is listed as an 'independent pass' to batch via the compose-only seam, but the link write path goes through `rebar.graph._links.add_dependency` → `_write_link_event` → `_seam.append_event`, NOT through `composer.create_core`/`append_event`. `add_dependency` performs stateful reads (cycle detection, hierarchy promotion, idempotency check) before writing — all requiring committed state. The plan's compose-only seam targets only `create_core`/`append_event` and does not enumerate `graph._links` as an edit target or explain how `link` calls are batched.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Independent passes only: Pass 1 (CREATE) and Passes 2a–2d (parents/links/file-impact/comments)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py` to the edit-target list with compose-only variants for `comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`, OR explain how Passes 2c/2d events are buffered without modifying `leaf.py` (e.g., by intercepting at the `append_event` layer in `_seam.py`).
findings.finding: The compose-only seam is described as a variant of `create_core`/`append_event`, but Passes 2c (file_impact, verify_commands) and 2d (comments) route through `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py` (`set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`, `comment`) — each calling `append_event` directly. These are unenumerated edit targets: to batch Pass 2c and 2d events, either `leaf.py` also needs compose-only variants, or the plan must explain an alternative routing strategy. `leaf.py` is not listed in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: Scope section: 'buffer composed events per pass and flush through `batch_stage_and_commit`'; import_ndjson.py Pass 2c and 2d

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify and fix Pass 2b batching: either (a) explicitly scope `link` events out of the batch path (keeping them per-event like Pass 2e) and update AC2 accordingly, or (b) specify how `add_dependency`'s state-reading guards (cycle detection, reciprocal `relates_to` write, `_is_active_link`) will work correctly in a compose-only model before any batch events are committed.
findings.finding: Pass 2b (links) cannot be routed through a compose-only seam without bypassing `add_dependency`'s state-reading guards: cycle detection, hierarchy resolution, idempotency check (`_is_active_link`), and the conditional reciprocal LINK write for `relates_to` all read committed on-disk state. A compose-only variant for links either skips these guards (incorrect behavior) or runs them against pre-batch state (stale, missing events composed but not yet committed), producing incorrect cycle detection and missed reciprocal links.
findings.location: Scope — 'Independent passes only: Pass 1 (CREATE) and Passes 2a–2d (parents/links/file-impact/comments)'; src/rebar/graph/_links.py::add_dependency (lines 142–271)
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest correct fix is to treat Pass 2b (links) like Pass 2e — keep it per-event — because `add_dependency`'s guard logic is inherently stateful against committed disk state. Update AC2 to read: 'Importer Passes 1, 2c, 2d buffer composed events and flush via batch_stage_and_commit in 256-event chunks; Pass 2b remains per-event alongside Pass 2e due to stateful cycle/idempotency guards.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge and justify overriding the `docs/scale-envelope.md` standing decision ('intentionally no batched-commit fast path') by scoping the batch path to import-only and updating that doc to reflect the new tradeoff.
findings.finding: docs/scale-envelope.md (line 43) explicitly records 'there is intentionally no batched-commit fast path (it would weaken the per-write durability guarantee)' as a standing design decision. The plan introduces exactly this pattern without citing, acknowledging, or arguing against that standing decision.
findings.location: ## Why — 'each pass still calls rebar.create_ticket/edit_ticket/link/comment … one lock + one commit per call. That per-event seam is the real bottleneck. Routing the independent passes through B1's batch_stage_and_commit collapses their commits.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section 'Prior art / design override' that cites docs/scale-envelope.md, explains why the import-only scope makes the durability tradeoff acceptable (a re-run is idempotent, mid-batch crash leaves no committed partial state), and commits to updating that doc as part of this story's AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm `batch_stage_and_commit` is delivered and merged before this story begins (with a ticket/story link), or document why the simpler alternative (keep per-event commits, leverage the existing `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` deferral already in the importer) is insufficient with measured evidence.
findings.finding: The plan's prerequisite `batch_stage_and_commit` (the 'B1 batch primitive') has zero existing call-sites and does not exist in the codebase at all — the plan is authored as if it already exists ('Adopt the B1 batch primitive'), but it is itself an unbuilt, unverified artifact. No prior-art search or alternative was considered for achieving bulk-import commit reduction without a new batch primitive (e.g., the already-present `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` + single final `push_tickets_branch`, which the importer already uses).
findings.location: ## What — 'Adopt the B1 batch primitive on the NDJSON importer's independent passes so a bulk import commits ceil(events/chunk) commits instead of one-per-event.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand Scope to address the `add_dependency`/`_write_link_event` capability gap: either (a) add a compose-only fork in `src/rebar/graph/_links.py` that returns the LINK event dict without committing, or (b) explicitly exclude Pass 2b from AC2's batching claim and document it as per-event (like Pass 2e).
findings.finding: Pass 2b (links) cannot be routed through the compose-only seam and batch_stage_and_commit without a compose-only fork inside `add_dependency`/`_write_link_event`, which is absent from the Scope.
findings.location: Scope — 'Add a compose-only seam: a variant of `create_core`/`append_event`'; AC2 — 'Importer Passes 1 + 2a–2d buffer composed events and flush via `batch_stage_and_commit`'
findings.suggested_fix: The simpler fix is (b): move Pass 2b (links) to the per-event group alongside Pass 2e in the AC, and update the Why/Scope to note that link events cannot be batched without refactoring add_dependency's internal write path. Links are typically far fewer than creates/comments, so the commit-reduction benefit of batching them is marginal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) that requires an integration-level import test against a real (temporary) git repository, asserting both the commit count (using git rev-list) and the replayed state hash — so the batch cutover is exercised end-to-end, not only through mocked layers.
findings.finding: The cutover of Passes 1 and 2a–2d to batch_stage_and_commit has no acceptance criterion that exercises the new code path end-to-end (live commits produced), satisfying E6f — `make test` alone cannot prove commit-count reduction if the test suite mocks the git layer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 + AC6; Scope — 'buffer composed events and flush through batch_stage_and_commit'
findings.suggested_fix: AC4 (extended): 'Running `python -m rebar.io.import_ndjson --file tests/fixtures/import_512.ndjson` against a temporary real repo produces ≤3 commits (ceil(512/256)+1 for Pass 2e), verified by `git -C $TMPDIR rev-list --count HEAD`; replayed state matches reference via sha256sum.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit error-handling clause: specify whether a failed chunk raises immediately (abandoning remaining buffer) and confirm that source_id idempotency guarantees a clean re-run from any mid-flush failure point.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling when batch_stage_and_commit fails mid-chunk (e.g., git lock contention, disk full): it is unclear whether the partially-staged chunk is rolled back, whether the in-memory buffer is safely abandoned, and whether a subsequent re-run can cleanly recover via source_id re-scan from that intermediate state.
findings.location: Scope: 'buffer composed events per pass and flush through batch_stage_and_commit in chunks'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the Scope section how ticket_id is assigned in the compose-only seam for CREATE events (e.g. 'ticket_id is a UUID generated in-process before staging; identical to how create_core currently allocates it').
findings.finding: The compose-only seam for CREATE (Pass 1) must assign a ticket_id before committing, but the plan does not specify how ticket_id is generated without a write — leaving a non-trivial design choice to the executor.
findings.location: Scope — 'Add a compose-only seam: a variant of create_core/append_event that RETURNS the composed (ticket_id, event_dict) WITHOUT committing'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief benchmark or spike (e.g. import a representative NDJSON fixture at chunk sizes 64/256/1024) to confirm 256 is a reasonable default before hardcoding it permanently.
findings.finding: The chunk size of 256 is asserted as a hard-coded default with no measurement rationale or benchmark plan to validate it is appropriate for the expected import sizes.
findings.location: Scope section: 'flush through `batch_stage_and_commit` in chunks (default 256, hard-coded — no new config key)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run a quick benchmark import (e.g. 1 000-event fixture) measuring wall-clock time and commit count at chunk sizes 64, 256, and 1 024; record the result in a comment next to the constant so future maintainers understand the derivation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to update CLAUDE.md (or an ADR) documenting the compose-only seam pattern: its purpose, its interface, and the rule that stateful passes must remain per-event.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — a compose-only (non-committing) seam splitting the write path — with no mention of capturing the decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Add a compose-ONLY seam: a variant of `create_core`/`append_event` that RETURNS the composed `(ticket_id, event_dict)` WITHOUT committing'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to CLAUDE.md under 'Architecture' describing the compose/commit split: what the seam returns, which callers should use each variant, and why Pass 2e is excluded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: at minimum, log chunk flush progress (chunk N of M, events committed) and surface batch-flush errors with enough context to diagnose the failure.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new buffering and batch-flush layer but specifies no observability (logging, metrics, or trace points) for flush progress, chunk commit counts, or flush failures — operators have no visibility into whether the batch path is working or why an import stalled.
findings.location: Scope / AC list — no observability requirement present
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Each chunk flush logs its index, event count, and outcome (success/error) at DEBUG level; a flush error logs the exception and re-raises.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC covering failure behavior in the new buffering path: specify what happens (skip-and-log, abort-chunk, or full-rollback) when composition fails for a record mid-buffer, and verify that the error is surfaced rather than silently swallowed.
findings.finding: The ACs for the new buffering/flushing logic are happy-path only — no AC addresses failure handling when composition or commit fails mid-buffer (e.g., what happens to the partial buffer, whether events already staged are rolled back or committed, whether the importer reports a clear error).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Importer Passes 1 + 2a–2d buffer composed events and flush via batch_stage_and_commit in 256-event chunks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'If composition of an event fails within a buffer, the importer logs the error with source_id context, skips that event (or aborts the chunk), and the remaining events are not silently lost; a test confirms that a deliberately-malformed record mid-buffer does not cause silent partial commit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the compose-only seam (returning `(ticket_id, event_dict)` without committing) has ≥2 additional callers beyond the B2 importer; if not, consider inlining the composition logic directly into the importer loop rather than adding a general-purpose seam to `composer.py`/`_seam.py`.
findings.finding: The new compose-only seam (a variant of `create_core`/`append_event` that returns `(ticket_id, event_dict)` without committing) is introduced with exactly one consumer named in the plan — the B2 importer. No grep hits for any equivalent function or any other caller exist in `src/`. This fails the Rule-of-Three for a new abstraction (1 call-site, not ≥3), making it a premature abstraction as specified by A1.
findings.location: Scope — 'Add a compose-ONLY seam: a variant of `create_core`/`append_event` that RETURNS the composed `(ticket_id, event_dict)` WITHOUT committing (`src/rebar/_commands/composer.py`, `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address HLC timestamp ordering for intra-batch events on the same ticket: either document that the global HLC cache (not the per-ticket witness) provides ordering within a single import process and accept the weaker cross-clone guarantee, or assign timestamps inside the locked batch commit (after staging but before the git commit) so `_max_event_prefix` witnesses each prior event's file in sequence.
findings.finding: The compose-only seam assigns HLC timestamps at compose time, before any batch events are committed to disk. For a ticket receiving multiple events in the same pass (e.g., multiple COMMENTs in Pass 2d), `hlc.next_tick` for event N cannot witness event N-1's filename prefix (not yet on disk), weakening the per-ticket causal floor guarantee. The global HLC cache may maintain ordering in the single-import-process case, but the cross-clone monotonicity property (which relies on `_max_event_prefix` reading committed files) is not preserved for intra-batch events on the same ticket.
findings.location: Scope — 'buffer composed events per pass and flush through batch_stage_and_commit in chunks'; src/rebar/_store/hlc.py::next_tick (line 140–159); src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py::append_event (line 182)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document whether HLC timestamps must be generated inside the write lock (at commit time, not compose time) for the compose-only seam to preserve the store's HLC monotonicity invariant, and adjust the seam design accordingly.
findings.finding: The compose-only seam buffers events with HLC timestamps generated at composition time (inside `append_event`'s `hlc.next_tick` call), but the events are committed later in batch. If clock drift or HLC monotonicity violations occur between composition and commit time for events across multiple tickets, the timestamp ordering guarantee in the committed store may be violated — an unverified capability assertion about the HLC's correctness across a deferred commit window.
findings.location: Scope — 'Add a compose-ONLY seam: a variant of create_core/append_event that RETURNS the composed (ticket_id, event_dict) WITHOUT committing'
findings.suggested_fix: Generate the HLC timestamp inside `batch_stage_and_commit` (at lock-acquisition time) rather than in the compose step, passing a placeholder or deferring `hlc.next_tick` until the lock is held.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how source_id idempotency is enforced across the compose→flush gap (e.g., optimistic lock on flush, process-level mutex, or document that concurrent imports of the same file are unsupported/guarded at the call site).
findings.finding: Concurrent import processes can both pass the source_id idempotency check before either commits, leading to duplicate events: the compose-only seam defers all commits to flush time, creating a TOCTOU window where a second concurrent importer reads source_id state that is stale (none of the buffered events have landed yet).
findings.location: Scope / compose-only seam + AC: 'Idempotent re-import (source_id re-scan) still skips already-imported events'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale paragraph explaining why the compose-only seam boundary was chosen (e.g., 'keeps all validation in the command layer, lets `batch_stage_and_commit` stay a pure store primitive') so implementers understand the design intent when adapting the approach to stateful callers like `add_dependency`.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing the compose-only seam approach over structurally different alternatives. It describes the mechanism and the problem it solves, but omits why this interface boundary (compose → buffer → batch-commit) was preferred.
findings.location: Why section; Scope section — no 'why this approach' prose

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
